title: SDN Controller Health
dataSources: SDN controller system logs, cluster status API
app: Custom scripted input, SDN controller syslog
spl:
index=sdn sourcetype="sdn:controller"
| search (cluster_state OR heartbeat OR leader_election OR consensus)
| eval health=case(
    searchmatch("healthy") OR searchmatch("active"), "Healthy",
    searchmatch("degraded") OR searchmatch("standby"), "Degraded",
    searchmatch("failed") OR searchmatch("unreachable"), "Critical",
    1==1, "Unknown")
| stats latest(health) as status, latest(_time) as last_heartbeat by controller_id, role
| table controller_id, role, status, last_heartbeat
description: SDN controllers are the brain of software-defined networks. Controller outages or cluster consensus failures can cause network-wide disruption. Monitoring controller health ensures the control plane remains available and consistent.
value: SDN controllers are the brain of software-defined networks. Controller outages or cluster consensus failures can cause network-wide disruption. Monitoring controller health ensures the control plane remains available and consistent.
implementation: Monitor SDN controller cluster via heartbeat polling every 15 seconds. Track cluster membership, leader election events, and consensus state. Alert immediately on controller failure or split-brain conditions. Monitor controller resource utilization (CPU, memory, database size). Maintain runbook for controller failover.
visualization: Status grid (controller cluster), Timeline (cluster events), Single value (cluster health), Table (controller details).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Custom scripted input, SDN controller syslog.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: SDN controller system logs, cluster status API.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Monitor SDN controller cluster via heartbeat polling every 15 seconds. Track cluster membership, leader election events, and consensus state. Alert immediately on controller failure or split-brain conditions. Monitor controller resource utilization (CPU, memory, database size). Maintain runbook for controller failover.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=sdn sourcetype="sdn:controller"
| search (cluster_state OR heartbeat OR leader_election OR consensus)
| eval health=case(
    searchmatch("healthy") OR searchmatch("active"), "Healthy",
    searchmatch("degraded") OR searchmatch("standby"), "Degraded",
    searchmatch("failed") OR searchmatch("unreachable"), "Critical",
    1==1, "Unknown")
| stats latest(health) as status, latest(_time) as last_heartbeat by controller_id, role
| table controller_id, role, status, last_heartbeat
```

Understanding this SPL

**SDN Controller Health** — SDN controllers are the brain of software-defined networks. Controller outages or cluster consensus failures can cause network-wide disruption. Monitoring controller health ensures the control plane remains available and consistent.

Documented **Data sources**: SDN controller system logs, cluster status API. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Custom scripted input, SDN controller syslog. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: sdn; **sourcetype**: sdn:controller. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=sdn, sourcetype="sdn:controller". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **health** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by controller_id, role** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Pipeline stage (see **SDN Controller Health**): table controller_id, role, status, last_heartbeat


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm results in the index, then compare with the same scope in the relevant fabric/SDN controller or manager UI.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Status grid (controller cluster), Timeline (cluster events), Single value (cluster health), Table (controller details).
grandmaExplanation: SDN controllers are the brain of software-defined networks — so traffic problems, routing mistakes, and risky changes surface fast.
knownFalsePositives: Faults during planned maintenance, hardware refresh, or after firmware upgrades.
